headings: h3: We need your help to continue fundraising
homepage.text_excerpt: McConnell International Foundation – Challenging issues of poverty and conflict throughout the world Skip to content Home Who We Are Menu Toggle Our Vision Our Trustees What We Do Menu Toggle Keep Girls in School Previous Projects Supporting Children Livingstone Volunteers News Fundraising Contact Us Donate Donate Main Menu Home Who We Are Menu Toggle Our Vision Our Trustees What We Do Menu Toggle Keep Girls in School Previous Projects Supporting Children Livingstone Volunteers News Fundraising Contact Us Donate Donate We believe that in this interdependent world we must, and we can all make a difference Challenging issues of poverty and conflict throughout the world Read More OUR VISION The McConnell International Foundation exists to support those who are working to build peace and sustainable development, particularly but not exclusively in sub-Saharan Africa. We work with local partners to deliver innovative programmes within our priorities of conflict prevention and peacebuilding, education and skills development. Peace and sustainable development go hand in hand. Those people living in places dominated by violent conflict are least likely to go to school, earn an income or enjoy basic rights. Over 70 million people are displaced worldwide and not only do they experience deprivation but they are ripe for exploitation too. We recognise the particular challenges faced by vulnerable teenagers and we invest in the empowerment of girls and young women as a priority. Our Foundation fundamentally believes that in this interdependent world we must and we can all make a difference. We are determined to be agents of sustainable change and we are grateful to all those who have supported our work and who will do so in the future. What We Do The McConnell International Foundation prioritises education, good governance and peacebuilding. Fundamentally, we believe that every child deserves the best possible start in life with the opportunity to learn, participate and be free from fear. MIF identifies opportunities to help fill gaps in provision or support innovative local approaches to conflict prevention, peace building and development. We are supported by regular donors, occasional appeals, grants and by our own efforts. We fundamentally believe in working with local partners or trusted international organisations who have a record of supporting children in the right way at the right time in the right place. Read More NEWS Keeping Girls in School - Scholarship Scheme These scholarships will target girls who need support most in Malawi’s rural districts. The pandemic lockdowns must not lead to girls leaving school forever. We are excited to be able to help these girls find hope again. – Lord Jack McConnell, Chair, McConnell International Foundation Read More Donate We need your help to continue fundraising If you would like to support our work please consider donating through our JustGiving site. pages.title: Livingstone Volunteers – McConnell International Foundation
pages.text_excerpt: Livingstone Volunteers – McConnell International Foundation Skip to content Home Who We Are Menu Toggle Our Vision Our Trustees What We Do Menu Toggle Keep Girls in School Previous Projects Supporting Children Livingstone Volunteers News Fundraising Contact Us Donate Donate Main Menu Home Who We Are Menu Toggle Our Vision Our Trustees What We Do Menu Toggle Keep Girls in School Previous Projects Supporting Children Livingstone Volunteers News Fundraising Contact Us Donate Donate Personal or financial challenges should not exclude young people from volunteering Livingstone Volunteers The McConnell International Foundation believes that international volunteering can help Scottish youngsters widen their horizons, grow in confidence and understand the world a bit better. But not all young people have the opportunity to reap the benefits of volunteering abroad, particularly where the financial contribution required is substantial. In 2014, the Scottish Government financially supported a number of events to promote the legacy of David Livingstone, the 19th century missionary and explorer who played a pivotal in ending to the slave trade in East Africa, including Malawi. MIF successfully bid for one of the grants available and decided to establish an annual scheme that would help youngsters from Scotland who were facing disadvantages or challenges take part in volunteering opportunities in the countries most associated with Livingstone – Malawi, Zambia, Tanzania, and South Africa. The Foundation has established the Livingstone Volunteers scheme to help support the fundraising efforts of those young Scots who have the chance to volunteer in Africa but are struggling to meet the costs and run the scheme annually between 2014 and 2019. We were supported annually by the Scottish Government, the Sir Tom Hunter Foundation and a number of donors. Due to the Covid-19 pandemic and the restrictions placed upon international travel, the Trustees of the McConnell International Foundation took the difficult decision in early 2020 to suspend the Livingstone Volunteers. We will take the time available to review the scheme and modernise it for a post-Covid world. As part of this, we plan to initiate a forum with other partners on the ‘Future of Global Volunteering’. Should you wish to be involved in this forum, or for more information, please contact us on mcconnellintfoundation@gmail.com . Life changing opportunities 2014-2019 Livingstone Volunteers 2018 The fifth anniversary of the Livingstone Volunteers Scheme, and it did not disappoint. Thank you to all of our wonderful grant recipients, judging panel, and our supporters and donors. Want to learn what the Scheme is all about? Check out the 2018 report. Livingstone Volunteers 2017 Congratulations to all of our recipients, we were delighted to have you represent the scheme. To learn more about what our volunteers got up to, take a look at the 2017 report . Livingstone Volunteers 2016 The 2016 Livingstone Volunteers scheme was launched in October 2015. The 2016 scheme saw the highest number of full £500 grants being awarded in Livingstone Volunteers’ history and awarded these grants to young persons across Scotland, from Glasgow to Stirling to Fife to Aberdeen. The summary report on the Livingstone Volunteers 2016 is now able to view . Livingstone Volunteers 2015 The 2015 scheme focused on young people aged from 15 to 19 years who secured a volunteer placement on their school’s 2015 trip to sub-Saharan Eastern Africa, or with a similar organised group. Livingstone Volunteers 2015 saw a total of 20 applications, with 18 grants being awarded. An event was held in April 2015 to present each of the successful applicants with their grant. To read all about how are volunteers got on, visit the full report of the 2015 Scheme . pages.text_excerpt: News – McConnell International Foundation Skip to content Home Who We Are Menu Toggle Our Vision Our Trustees What We Do Menu Toggle Keep Girls in School Previous Projects Supporting Children Livingstone Volunteers News Fundraising Contact Us Donate Donate Main Menu Home Who We Are Menu Toggle Our Vision Our Trustees What We Do Menu Toggle Keep Girls in School Previous Projects Supporting Children Livingstone Volunteers News Fundraising Contact Us Donate Donate News Our review of Keep Girls in School 2025 Catch up on all our news with our annual review which has just been published. Thanks to all our supporters who made 2025 our most successful year yet. Your generosity, advice and support means that 200 girls completed another year of secondary education – changing their lives for the better. 2025 Annual Report Keep Girls in School 2025: A second chance for girls to help them beat the odds This was a big year for many of the 200 girls that we support through our Keep Girls in School Programme. The 100 students in the Ntichisi and Zomba Rural districts sat their national Junior Certificate of Education (JCE) exams in June, which a girls must pass if she is to progress to Year 3. The results arrived in August, just before the new school year began on September 22 and once again the MIF scholars far exceeded the national average pass rate. Ninety-five girls sat the JCE exams. The results showed that 88 girls passed – 89 per cent of our scholars, compared to a national pass rate for girls of 73.5 per cent. The overall national pass rate was 77.6 per cent). We are delighted to say that the seven girls who failed have been given a second chance to repeat Year 2 and resit the JCE exams next June. This new programme means that girls who fail their JCE exam will not have to leave school at the end of second year. Of the five who did not sit the exams, one girl had moved to another school earlier in the school year and four were absent because they had dropped out. Of the four girls who dropped out of school, one was from Malomo CDSS and three from its neighbouring school Kaulatsitsi CDSS. This is a 4 percent drop-out, compared to the annual average rate of 10 per cent (UNICEF).It is a new school, only opened four years ago, and is situated in in a very conservative rural community where traditional beliefs about women and girls still hold sway. The headteacher Ellen Chinawaexplained that there were two issues at play: secondary education was not yet embedded in the local culture and girls are expected to give up education at the end of primary school as there is no value attached to a secondary education. She explained that when the school opened there were 30 girls in year 1, of that cohort there are now only five in Year 4.Our KGIS scholars are changing these outcomes. The Kaulatsitsi pass rate of 18 girls out of 25 is 72 per cent, very close to the national average and there will be 25 girls in Year 2 and 3 supported by KCA and MIF – the entire year 3 cohort (21 girls) and 4 in Year 2 (repeating). MIF scholars at Kaulatsitsi CDSS, October 2025 Our Malawi partner, WOLREC with support from the Foundation and our partner Keep A Child Alive and working with the headteacher and community leaders will deliver a sensitisation programme for parents and guardians early in 2026, to help them understand the benefits of keeping girls in school. The other cohort of 100 girls are at schools in Balaka and Salima Districts. These girls range from year 1 students to year 4. This year 28 girls sat their national Malawi Certificate of Secondary Education exams (MSCE). with 12 passing. This is a pass rate of 43 per cent compared to the national pass rate for girls of 53 per cent and an overall pass rate of 58 per cent. The girls who failed have all been given the chance to repeat year 4 so that they may resit their MSCE exams in July 2026. Margret (L) with Debora. Two of the students who will repeat Year 4 thanks to the Foundation’s Second Chance to L
